serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of honey milk tea (less sugar) is approximately 250 ml, providing around 200 kcal and 29.6 g of sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Milk Tea (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Honey Milk Tea (Less Sugar), cr_id D08010054, with confidence 0.95. All per_100g values preserved from the canonical HPB source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for honey milk tea (less sugar) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low, per the standard classification boundary of ≤55 = low) based on analogous sweetened milk tea entries and honey GI values (honey GI ~55–58, University of Sydney GI Database). A GI of exactly 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55). Carbohydrate per serving = 14.73 g/100g × 250 g serving = 36.8 g. GL = (55 × 36.8) / 100 = 20.2. This is a conservative estimate reflecting the combined effect of honey sugars (fructose + glucose) and milk lactose in a liquid matrix, which accelerates glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 11.83 g sugar/100 g exceeding the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold; sodium is low at 13 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Despite low sodium content, the high sugar load (11.83 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage, which independently elevates blood pressure risk; individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this drink and opt for unsweetened tea alternatives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (11.83 g/100 g) from honey and added sugar in liquid form with negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g), causing rapid glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high liquid sugar content from honey and added sweeteners, which causes rapid postprandial glucose spikes; unsweetened tea without honey is strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sugar intake from sweetened beverage associated with increased triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; fat content (2.41 g/100 g) may include saturated fat from non-dairy creamer
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages such as honey milk tea is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular disease risk; individuals with CVD should limit intake and request unsweetened or low-fat alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (11.83 g/100 g) with no anti-inflammatory nutrients; potential saturated fat from non-dairy creamer
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high sugar content of this beverage may promote pro-inflammatory mediators relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with RA should avoid sugar-sweetened beverages and choose unsweetened tea with anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Milk Tea (Less Sugar) is a freshly prepared Singaporean speciality tea beverage made by combining brewed black tea with milk and a reduced quantity of honey as the primary sweetener. It is commonly sold at bubble tea shops, kopitiams, and hawker stalls across Singapore. The drink has a smooth, mildly sweet flavour with floral honey notes and a creamy texture from the milk component, which may be fresh milk or non-dairy creamer depending on the vendor. Despite the 'less sugar' designation, the beverage still contains 11.83 g of sugar per 100 g, classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB and WHO guidelines. Per 100 g, it provides 80 kcal of energy, 0.34 g protein, 2.41 g fat, 14.73 g carbohydrate, 11.83 g sugar, 0.03 g dietary fibre, and 13 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low GI boundary) based on analogous honey and sweetened milk tea data from the University of Sydney GI Database, with a glycaemic load of approximately 20.2 per 250 ml serving. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to rapid liquid sugar absorption from honey and added sweeteners. It also poses moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its SSB classification and potential saturated fat contribution from non-dairy creamer. It is best consumed occasionally and in small quantities; unsweetened tea is preferred for health-conscious individuals.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Honey Milk Tea (Less Sugar), cr_id D08010054, with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean foods.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this freshly prepared honey milk tea (less sugar) are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D08010054 as of 2026. Calcium (3.0 mg/100 g) and iron (0.15 mg/100 g) are reported by HPB and preserved. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on honey (GI ~55–58) and sweetened milk tea entries from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for this specific product. A GI of 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low GI classification (≤55 = low); gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Dietary fibre is 0.03 g/100 g (effectively trace). The 'less sugar' designation reduces honey quantity relative to standard preparation but does not eliminate added sugar; the product remains classified as an SSB at 11.83 g sugar/100 g.
